messages.stats.health.summary: Fast admin snapshot from recent app usage, updated from background telemetry.
messages.stats.graph.action.activate.bridges: Raise bridge-user and inside-network introduction multipliers.
messages.stats.graph.action.balance.clusters: Balance cluster-quality and weak-tie multipliers; avoid isolating good circles.
messages.stats.graph.action.break.cliques: Lower same-cluster priority and raise cross-community suggestion weight.
messages.stats.graph.action.collect.evidence: Keep multipliers neutral until more ratings, invites, and event participation arrive.
messages.stats.graph.action.grow.recurring: Boost recurring-event formats and repeated-encounter signals.
messages.stats.graph.action.improve.quality: Increase affinity, attendance, and feedback confidence multipliers.
messages.stats.graph.action.increase.reach: Raise friends-of-friends and mixed-community exposure multipliers.
messages.stats.graph.action.keep.balance: Keep current multipliers; the graph balance looks healthy.
messages.stats.graph.action.open.discovery: Raise weak-tie and outside-network exploration multipliers.
messages.stats.graph.actions.title: Tuning suggestions
messages.stats.graph.advisory.action: Action rule: high clustering needs cross-circle suggestions; low reach needs bridge users and mixed events.
messages.stats.graph.advisory.body: This is social topology, not click analytics. It shows whether people are connected through useful bridges, weak ties, and repeat encounters.
messages.stats.graph.advisory.clustering: Clustering shows whether circles are healthy or too closed.
messages.stats.graph.advisory.reach: 2-hop reach shows how much of the city is reachable through friends of friends.
messages.stats.graph.advisory.recurring: Recurring edges show real community forming.
messages.stats.graph.advisory.title: How to read it
messages.stats.graph.advisory.weak.ties: Weak ties open discovery beyond direct friends.
messages.stats.graph.help.actions.body: Use the state as an operating guide: high clustering means add cross-circle suggestions, low reach means prioritize bridge users, low weak ties means show new faces, and low recurring ties means create repeatable events.
messages.stats.graph.help.actions.title: What to do with it
messages.stats.graph.help.bridges.body: A bridge person connects otherwise separate circles. If bridges disappear, the graph can fragment even when total activity looks fine.
messages.stats.graph.help.bridges.title: Bridge people
messages.stats.graph.help.clustering.body: Clustering shows how closed the local circles are. Too high can create cliques; too low can mean people do not feel belonging.
messages.stats.graph.help.clustering.title: Clustering
messages.stats.graph.help.edges.body: Users are nodes and edges are real connection evidence: ratings, affinity, invitations, event overlap, or repeated encounters.
messages.stats.graph.help.edges.title: Users and edges
messages.stats.graph.help.evolution.body: Graph evolution shows whether new, weak, and recurring edges are growing over time. This is the living health of the community.
messages.stats.graph.help.evolution.title: Graph evolution
messages.stats.graph.help.health.body: Graph health combines reachability, bridge coverage, weak ties, clustering, recurring ties, and edge quality into one operating score.
messages.stats.graph.help.health.title: Graph health
messages.stats.graph.help.intro: This is not classic engagement analytics. It explains the shape of the social network: who connects circles, how far discovery reaches, and whether first meetings become community.
messages.stats.graph.help.reach.body: 2-hop reach asks how much of a city can be reached through a friend of a friend. High reach means discovery can grow without everybody knowing everybody.
messages.stats.graph.help.reach.title: 2-hop reach
messages.stats.graph.help.recurring.body: Recurring ties show repeated interaction. They are usually a stronger community signal than one-off clicks or first meetings.
messages.stats.graph.help.recurring.title: Recurring ties
messages.stats.graph.help.title: How to read the social graph
messages.stats.graph.help.weak.body: Weak ties are light connections outside the close circle. They are valuable because they open new people, events, and communities.
messages.stats.graph.help.weak.title: Weak ties
messages.stats.graph.insight.bridge-risk: There are not enough bridge people for the current graph size. Mixed events and inside-network introductions should help clusters connect without forcing random exposure.
messages.stats.graph.insight.cliques: The graph is becoming clique-heavy. Suggestions should lean toward weak ties and cross-community events so discovery does not collapse into closed circles.
messages.stats.graph.insight.closed: The graph has too few weak ties. Outside-network and friends-of-friends suggestions should get more room until discovery opens up.
messages.stats.graph.insight.cold: Not enough graph evidence yet. Keep collecting ratings, event attendance, and invitations before trusting the social graph signals.
messages.stats.graph.insight.fragmented: Reachability is low, so users sit in separated islands. Prioritize bridge users, mixed events, and suggestions that connect nearby communities.
messages.stats.graph.insight.healthy: The graph has useful bridges, enough weak ties, and stable recurring edges. Keep the current balance between belonging and discovery.
messages.stats.graph.map.bridge.legend: Small dark circles = connectors; number shows connector strength.
messages.stats.graph.map.community.legend: Big circles = user circles; number shows people in that circle.
messages.stats.graph.subtitle: Materialized social graph science from ratings, affinity edges, invitations, and event membership.
messages.stats.graph.timeline.title: Graph evolution
messages.stats.graph.title: Social graph
